title: Units and Measurement
subtitle: Chapter 1: Units and Measurement — NEB Class 11

content.text: Physical quantity: Anything that can be measured (length, mass, time, temperature, etc.).
Fundamental quantities: 7 base quantities in SI system.
Derived quantities: Obtained by combining fundamental ones (e.g., velocity, force, energy).
Supplementary quantities: Plane angle (radian, rad) and solid angle (steradian, sr).
SI (Système International d'Unités) is the internationally accepted metric system. Advantages: coherent, decimal-based, universally accepted, and easy conversion.

content.name: SI Base Units
content.formula: \text{Length: m, Mass: kg, Time: s, Temp: K, Current: A, Substance: mol, Intensity: cd}
content.note: Standard international units for base quantities.

content.text: Dimension: Expresses a quantity in terms of M (mass), L (length), T (time), I (current), $\theta$ (temperature), N (amount), and J (luminous intensity).
Principle of Homogeneity: Both sides of a physical equation must have the same dimensions.

content.name: Common Dimensional Formulas
content.formula: Force: $MLT^{-2}$ | Energy: $ML^2T^{-2}$ | Pressure: $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$ | Velocity: $LT^{-1}$ | Acceleration: $LT^{-2}$ | Momentum: $MLT^{-1}$
content.note: Essential for checking equation correctness and deriving relations.

content.text: Rules: All non-zero digits are significant; zeros between non-zeros are significant; trailing zeros after a decimal point are significant; leading zeros are NOT significant.
Systematic error: Consistent, one-directional error (caused by faulty instruments). Random error: Irregular, unpredictable error (reduced by averaging).

content.name: Error Expressions
content.formula: \text{Absolute error} = |\text{True} - \text{Measured}| \\ \text{Relative error} = \frac{\text{Absolute error}}{\text{True value}} \\ \text{Percentage error} = \text{Relative error} \times 100\%
content.note: Used to quantify uncertainty in measurements.
